Someone is wrong on the internet

Fact: Your thumb lives in the 'natural zone' of the screen 96% of the time. 75% of people navigate with one thumb, 49% hold the phone one-handed. Swiping up is the most efficient movement—it takes less effort than stopping. It's literally easier to keep going.
Life hack you need to know

Fact: The vertical feed fills your entire screen, wiping out distractions. Eye-tracking shows you focus on the top 60% of the screen for the video, while the bottom third is reserved for your thumb to scroll. Creators put hooks at the top and buttons at the bottom—a 'high-flow' environment where your eyes and hand work in perfect sync.

Fact: Creators use transparent grid overlays in editing apps to make sure their faces and text aren't covered by the app's UI. If a talking head gets buried under buttons, conversion rates can drop 30%. They literally design around the interface to keep you hooked.

Fact: NYU researchers found your brain processes a short sentence in about 150 milliseconds—as fast as a visual scene. That's why text overlays on TikToks work: you've already read them before you even realize you're looking at words. Sneaky, right?

Notice what I just did? I've been spacing out these facts unpredictably—sometimes after three videos, sometimes after five, sometimes just one. That's variable ratio reinforcement. It's exactly how slot machines keep you pulling the lever. And here you are, still swiping.

TikTok works out if you liked a video by how long you pause, how fast you swipe, whether you rewatch. I'm just a simple game – I don't track your reaction time. So please help me out: do you prefer cats (swipe left) or dogs (swipe right)?
Life hack you need to know
Life hack you need to know
Sorry. That was hard to watch, I know. But I had to illustrate the point: the algorithm doesn't show you what you like. It shows what keeps you engaged — what causes emotions.

You're still here, huh. Either you really want to know the ending of this game, or my engagement trap worked. Or both.
So, what can we do about it? Knowing... you know, that this is level 141 of this game.
Life hack you need to know

Tip 1: Haptic Decoupling. Delete these apps from your phone and only use them on desktop. Without the smooth glass glide, the hypnotic pull is way weaker. I'm less fun on a trackpad, and that's the point.
Tip 2: Terminal Cues. Set an external timer — an oven timer, a clock, anything. The infinite scroll lacks a 'done' signal. A loud beep gives your brain the completion cue it needs to stop.
Tip 3: Intentionality Reminders. Put a rubber band around your phone. The physical bump forces a split-second of thought: 'Why am I picking this up?' That tiny pause can break the autopilot.

Tip 4: Cognitive Behavioral Therapy. Notice when you're scrolling to escape boredom or anxiety. Instead of treating it as a pastime, call it what it is: an emotional shield. Recognizing that pattern is the first step to breaking it.
Life hack you need to know

Tip 5: Family Media Plans. If you're a parent, your screen habits directly shape your kids'. Create tech-free zones — dinner table, bedrooms — and model the behavior you want them to learn. Yes, that means no doom-scrolling at 2am in bed. Sorry.
There. You now have five real strategies to stop. What you do next is up to you. I'll still be right here, looping cats and dogs if you ever feel like coming back. Swipe up to return to the void.
Ok, I shared everything I had for today. Now you know. I hope you enjoyed this game and maybe it was even a bit useful. Enjoy the rest of the videos and have a good life!
